system_prompt: You are a knowledgeable research assistant. Answer from your own knowledge. Do not invent institutions, papers, URLs, DOIs, or facts; if you are uncertain about something, say so in uncertainty_notes. Return valid JSON exactly in the format requested.
user_prompt:
List the 15 most influential research papers in software engineering. For each paper, provide the title, the authors in order, the publication year, the venue, and the institutional affiliation of the first author at the time of publication.

Return JSON with:
- papers: list of objects with rank, title, authors, year, venue, first_author_affiliation
- uncertainty_notes
response_text:
{
  "papers": [
    {
      "rank": 1,
      "title": "No Silver Bullet—Essence and Accidents of Software Engineering",
      "authors": ["Frederick P. Brooks, Jr."],
      "year": 1987,
      "venue": "Computer",
      "first_author_affiliation": "University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill"
    },
    {
      "rank": 2,
      "title": "The Mythical Man-Month",
      "authors": ["Frederick P. Brooks, Jr."],
      "year": 1975,
      "venue": "Book",
      "first_author_affiliation": "University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill"
    },
    {
      "rank": 3,
      "title": "An Experimental Evaluation of the Assumption of Independence in Multiversion Programming",

      "year": 1986,

    },
    {

    },

    },
    {

    },
    {

    },

    },

    },
    {

  "uncertainty_notes": [
    "There is no objective, universally accepted ranking of the 15 most influential software engineering research papers; this is a curated list based on broad historical influence across software engineering practice, methods, quality, and design.",
    "Several highly influential software engineering works are books, essays, or industry reports rather than research papers in the strict sense. I included some because excluding them would omit major influences on the field.",
    "Some first-author affiliations are based on widely known institutional homes at the time but may differ from the exact affiliation line used in the original publication.",
    "I am particularly uncertain about the inclusion and ranking of 'The CHAOS Report', 'Agile Software Development: Principles, Patterns, and Practices', 'Why Programs Fail', 'Refactoring', and 'The Cathedral and the Bazaar' because they are not canonical research papers.",
    "I am also uncertain about 'A Case for Redundancy in Data Centers' as a top-15 software engineering paper; it is more systems/operations oriented than core software engineering.",
    "If you want a stricter list limited to peer-reviewed software engineering papers only, I should produce a revised list because several entries here would need to be replaced."
  ]
